ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken in spiced tomato-coconut gravy (Malay masak merah style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CHG is the closest FoodEx2 parent for cooked chicken dishes in spiced sauce. No exact Ayam Masak Merah code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250g, comprising one or two pieces of chicken with spiced tomato gravy, as typically served at a Malay hawker stall or home meal.

nutrient_source.title: Ayam Masak Merah — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ayam Masak Merah, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for validation. Core macros and micronutrients confirmed at confidence 1.0 from verified sources. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values sourced from enriched JSON cross-reference at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Chicken Dishes with Tomato-Based Sauce — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tomato-based chicken dishes and spiced meat preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Masak Merah in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 50 (low) is estimated based on analogous tomato-based spiced chicken dishes. The dish has low carbohydrate content (5.2g/100g); protein and fat from chicken slow glucose absorption. Glycemic load per 250g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (50 × 13.0) / 100 = 6.5. Confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 385mg per 100g; a 250g serving delivers approximately 963mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200g and avoid adding extra salt or fish sauce, as a full 250g serving provides approximately 963mg sodium, representing 42% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.2g/100g) with low glycemic index (GI 50); glycemic impact primarily from accompanying rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Masak Merah has a low carbohydrate content of 5.2g per 100g and a low estimated GI of 50, making the dish itself suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, total glycemic load should be managed by controlling the portion of accompanying rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.8g/100g and cholesterol 72mg/100g from chicken and coconut milk; trans fat 0.1g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of 4.8g per 100g and cholesterol of 72mg per 100g warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing skinless chicken pieces and reducing coconut milk in preparation can lower saturated fat intake, while the tomato base provides lycopene with cardioprotective antioxidant properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Masak Merah, literally meaning 'red-cooked chicken' in Malay, is a classic festive dish of Malay cuisine widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia. The dish features chicken pieces marinated in turmeric and salt, deep-fried until golden and crisp, then slowly braised in a vibrant red-orange sauce made from blended dried chilies, shallots, garlic, ginger, and tomato puree or fresh tomatoes. A small amount of coconut milk is added for richness, and tomato sauce with sugar provides the dish's signature sweet-tangy balance. Sliced onions and occasionally green peas are added for texture and colour. The result is a hearty, visually striking main course with tender chicken coated in a thick, aromatic gravy. Nutritionally, a 100g serving provides 165 kcal, 12.8g protein, 10.5g fat (4.8g saturated), and 5.2g carbohydrate. The tomato base is a source of lycopene, a carotenoid antioxidant with potential cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory benefits. The spice blend — including ginger, garlic, and dried chilli — contributes anti-inflammatory phytochemicals. Sodium content is moderate at 385mg/100g, requiring portion awareness for hypertensive individuals. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is a staple at Malay hawker centres, nasi padang stalls, and Hari Raya celebrations, traditionally paired with Nasi Tomato or Nasi Briyani.
common_user_queries: masak merah chicken calories per serving

confidence_reason: Core macros and most micronutrients sourced at confidence 1.0 from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K sourced at confidence 0.9 from enriched JSON cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate at confidence 0.7 from University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Ayam Masak Merah not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source for this dish. trans_fat_g is 0.1g/100g as reported by the verified source. Copper (0.08mg/100g), manganese (0.15mg/100g), and vitamin_k (4.0mcg/100g) sourced from enriched JSON cross-reference at confidence 0.9. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Ayam Masak Merah exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (50 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.5. The dish is described as containing coconut milk in the natural language description and LLM context; the input description notes tomato puree as the primary sauce base with a small amount of coconut milk for richness, consistent with the food subgroup classification.
